Q1: I tried to organize my studying around my work schedule, which honestly made things difficult. I'm 28 and working to support myself with limited resources, so I'd carve out time in the evenings and weekends, usually about 13 hours a week total. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd try to do practice problems and work through things physically rather than just reading. My routine was more about fitting study time wherever I could rather than having a perfect schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what was happening at work. Some weeks I could stick to a more regular evening routine, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram before deadlines. With 11 online courses to manage, I couldn't always give each one the consistent attention it deserved. I tried my best to stay steady, but life kept throwing curveballs.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due or when I had set aside specific study time. Sometimes I'd get email notifications about new materials or upcoming deadlines, and that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in regularly to watch the lecture videos since those were really important for understanding the material. Fear of falling behind was honestly a big motivator for me to keep checking.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in became part of my evening wind-down routine. I'd finish dinner, make some tea, and open up the LMS to see what needed attention. It helped that I had a consistent spot at my small desk where I'd always study. Once I associated that physical space with learning, opening the LMS felt more natural and automatic.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me because I could watch them multiple times and pause to take notes or work through problems myself. I watched almost 300 minutes of video content because that format really clicked with my kinesthetic learning style. The discussion posts were also helpful because interacting with peers helped me process ideas better. When I could connect concepts to real examples shared by classmates, things made more sense.
Q6: I tended to skip over some of the reading materials, especially long PDFs that felt dense and passive. With my learning style, just reading text doesn't stick as well, so I'd prioritize activities where I could do something. Some optional quizzes I also delayed because I was scared of seeing how little I actually understood. Time constraints meant I had to choose what to engage with deeply.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams, or when it involved actually doing something rather than passively consuming information. If I could practice a skill or work through a problem, I felt like I was making progress. Activities that seemed disconnected from assessments or too theoretical felt less valuable when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford. With required work already piling up and my limited time, optional things got pushed aside even when I knew they might help. There was this guilt about skipping them, but I had to prioritize what directly affected my grades. Required activities carried this weight of consequence that optional ones just didn't have.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my phone so I could review them during my commute or breaks at work. I also filled notebooks with handwritten notes and practice problems because writing things out helps me learn. A lot of my real learning happened away from the computer screen.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively studying but the LMS wouldn't capture it. I'd print out problem sets and work through them by hand at the library or at home. Sometimes I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times offline while taking notes. My peer study sessions happened over messaging apps, not through the LMS discussion boards, so that collaboration was invisible too.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy for the video content. I'd stream a lecture once to get the gist, then download it and watch it again offline while pausing to work through examples myself. The LMS would only show that initial viewing, but I might have engaged with that material three or four more times. It was the only way I could really absorb things with my learning style.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. My stress levels were already medium-high, and juggling 11 courses while working felt overwhelming. I remember feeling genuinely scared about falling behind and not being able to catch up. The CBT sessions I attended helped me manage the anxiety somewhat, but there were definitely weeks where I felt paralyzed and couldn't engage meaningfully with anything.
Q13: Definitely. When work got demanding or I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely sometimes. With low resources, I couldn't always afford good internet or a quiet place to study, which affected how much I could actually accomplish during my LMS sessions.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. There were nights when I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered because I was too tired to focus. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. My task completion rate looks decent at 81%, but honestly some of that was going through the motions rather than genuine engagement.
Q15: I postponed the more challenging assignments, the ones that scared me because I wasn't sure I understood the material well enough. Fear of failure made me avoid things even when I knew procrastinating would make it worse. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was fresher, but tomorrow would come with its own challenges. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but not always in time.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external structure since my internal motivation was low. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage intensively with the LMS. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more. They created these peaks of activity where I'd cram a lot of work into short periods, which wasn't ideal but kept me moving forward.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect my engagement, though not always positively. Good feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that topic or assignment type. My assignment scores weren't great, so feedback often highlighted gaps that felt overwhelming to address. I tried to use it constructively, but fear of more negative feedback sometimes made me hesitant.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to focus on when. Having videos, readings, and assignments organized in a predictable way made it easier to plan my limited study time. However, with 11 courses all structured differently, sometimes the competing structures created confusion about priorities. I appreciated when modules built on each other logically.
Q19: The video content being available for rewatching made engagement easier for someone like me who needs repetition. Discussion requirements pushed me to interact with peers, which was actually helpful. What made things harder was when too much content was released at once, or when instructions weren't clear about what was essential versus supplementary. My limited resources also meant technical difficulties sometimes blocked engagement.
Q20: I think that pattern describes someone who's trying but struggling, maybe like me in some ways. You might be logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions, but when it comes to assignments, the fear or confusion or competing demands prevent you from actually completing work. High activity can mask the fact that someone is spinning their wheels without making real progress toward submissions.
Q21: That makes sense to me because someone might have very efficient study strategies or strong background knowledge that lets them learn quickly without needing to spend hours in the LMS. Or they might be doing most of their real learning offline like I did. The LMS activity isn't the learning itself—it's just one pathway to learning. Some people find other pathways that work better for them.
Q22: A course log can accurately show when and how often someone accesses materials, and it can track completion of specific tasks. But it completely misses the quality of that engagement—whether someone was truly focused or just going through motions. It misses offline studying, emotional state, and the real cognitive work happening in someone's head. My 81% task completion looks okay, but it doesn't show how scared and stressed I was.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and apply what I'm learning. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about caring enough to wrestle with difficult concepts and connect them to what I already know. For me, engagement feels like being in a conversation with the material, asking questions, making mistakes, and trying again. It's hard to sustain when you're scared and stretched thin.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I cared about succeeding despite my struggles, or how hard I worked offline with my notebooks and downloaded materials. It wouldn't capture the fear that dominated my emotional experience or how the CBT sessions helped me cope. My peer interactions through messaging apps, my commute study sessions, my late nights handwriting practice problems—none of that appears in the data. The numbers tell a story, but it's not my whole story.
